topics.title: Develop Skill to Operate Two Roll Mills, Hydraulic Press, Hand Press, and Various Moulds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL508 - Polymer Processing Laboratory / Polymer Compounding & Processing Lab
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Develop Skill to Operate Two Roll Mills, Hydraulic Press, Hand Press, and Various Moulds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Polymer Processing Equipment — The "Big Four"

In any polymer or rubber compounding laboratory, the **machining backbone** consists of four critical units. The PCPOL508 lab course trains you to operate each of them safely and to interpret the processing parameters that govern the final product's quality.

### 1.1.1 Two Roll Mill (Open Mill / Mixing Mill)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Two Roll Mill** is a primary polymer/rubber processing machine consisting of two horizontally mounted, counter-rotating, heated rolls set at an adjustable **nip gap**, which uses high mechanical shear and friction to plasticize, mix, and homogenize polymer matrices with additives (curatives, fillers, plasticizers, accelerators).

- **Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** Imagine a giant, heated, motorized **pasta maker**. You feed cold dough (raw rubber + chemicals) into the two rotating cylinders, and the speed difference between the rolls *stretches, folds, and shears* the dough, mixing everything uniformly before it comes out as a smooth, homogeneous sheet.
- **Standard Lab Specifications:**
  - Roll Diameter: **150 mm – 250 mm**
  - Roll Length: **300 mm – 500 mm**
  - Friction Ratio: **1 : 1.1 to 1 : 1.4** (front roll slower, back roll faster)
  - Operating Temperature: **30 °C – 150 °C** (depending on polymer)
  - Drive: **5 HP – 25 HP** electric motor with gear reduction

### 1.1.2 Hydraulic Press (Compression Molding Press)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Hydraulic Press** is a compression molding machine that uses an incompressible fluid (hydraulic oil) driven by a pump to generate a high clamping/compressing force on a mould placed between heated platens, thereby shaping and curing thermoset polymers or vulcanizing rubber compounds under controlled **pressure (MPa)**, **temperature (°C)**, and **time (min)**.

- **Intuition:** Think of a **giant bottle jack** (the kind used to lift cars) connected to two heavy metal plates. The plates heat up (like a sandwich press), and you squeeze a mould containing polymer between them with thousands of kilograms of force.
- **Standard Lab Specifications:**
  - Clamping Force: **50 kN – 5000 kN (5 Ton – 500 Ton)**
  - Platen Size: **300 mm × 300 mm** to **600 mm × 600 mm**
  - Max Platen Temperature: **300 °C** (electrically heated)
  - Pressure Gauge: **0 – 35 MPa** analog / digital

### 1.1.3 Hand Press (Manual Molding Press / Laboratory Press)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Hand Press** is a manually operated, lever-and-screw driven compression press used in labs for small-scale molding, sample preparation, and teaching purposes where hydraulic power is not required.

- **Intuition:** Like a **heavy-duty garlic press** or a **vise grip** — you push down a long lever, and a screw mechanism multiplies your hand force into a much larger compression force on the mould.
- **Standard Lab Specifications:**
  - Capacity: **10 kN – 100 kN (1 Ton – 10 Ton)**
  - Operation: **Manual lever / Screw-jack**
  - Platen Size: **150 mm × 150 mm** to **300 mm × 300 mm**
  - Heating: Optional (electric cartridge heaters)

### 1.1.4 Various Moulds

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Moulds** are precision-machined, hardened steel (typically EN31 / H13 tool steel) cavities used to shape polymer melts into desired geometries under heat and pressure. They are classified by feeding mechanism: **Compression Mould**, **Transfer Mould**, and **Injection Mould**.

- **Intuition:** Moulds are the **"waffle iron"** of the polymer world — but instead of just shape, they control **flow direction, venting, surface finish, ejector pin placement, and flash line geometry**.

| Mould Type | Feeding Method | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| Compression Mould | Polymer placed directly in cavity | Rubber, thermosets, phenolic resins |
| Transfer Mould | Polymer forced from pot into cavity via plunger | Complex thermoset parts with inserts |
| Injection Mould | Molten polymer injected through sprue | Thermoplastics, mass production |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Focus:** The most commonly tested moulds are **dumbbell-shaped tensile specimen moulds (ASTM D412 / ISO 37)**, **disc-shaped moulds for hardness testing**, and **rectangular sheets for further characterization**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics Behind the "Big Four"

### 2.1.1 Two Roll Mill — Operating Principle

The mill works on three simultaneous physical phenomena:

1. **Friction (Adhesion to rolls):** The polymer sticks to the slower (front) roll due to higher frictional contact and is peeled off by the faster (back) roll, creating a **rolling bank** of material.
2. **Shear Deformation:** As the bank passes through the **nip gap** (typically **0.5 mm – 5 mm**), it is compressed and sheared. The shear rate can be extremely high.
3. **Heat Transfer:** Heated rolls (via internal steam/oil/electric heaters) conductively transfer thermal energy to the polymer, plasticizing it.

### 2.1.2 Hydraulic Press — Pascal's Law

The hydraulic press operates on **Pascal's Law** (1653):

> *Pressure applied to an enclosed, incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions.*

This means a small force on a small piston (pump) generates an equal pressure throughout the fluid, which acts on a much larger piston (ram) to multiply the force.

### 2.1.3 Hand Press — Mechanical Advantage

The hand press uses a **lever-and-screw** combination. The mechanical advantage is the product of the lever ratio and the screw thread pitch efficiency.

### 2.1.4 Mould Design Philosophy

A mould must balance:
- **Parting line location** (governs flash direction)
- **Venting** (allows trapped air/gas to escape)
- **Ejection mechanism** (ejector pins, plates)
- **Heating channels** (for uniform temperature distribution)

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 8 equations — they are the most frequently tested in KTU ESE and lab viva.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydraulic Pressure | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | MPa (N/mm²) | F = clamping force, A = plunger area |
| 2 | Clamping Force from Pressure | $F = P \times A$ | N or kN | Used to specify press tonnage |
| 3 | Friction Ratio (Two Roll Mill) | $FR = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | dimensionless | N₁ = back roll, N₂ = front roll (RPM) |
| 4 | Average Roll Surface Speed | $v = \pi \times D \times N$ | m/min | D = roll diameter, N = RPM |
| 5 | Mechanical Advantage (Hand Press) | $MA = \dfrac{L_{lever}}{r_{screw}} \times \dfrac{2\pi}{\tan(\alpha)}$ | dimensionless | Lever arm × screw efficiency |
| 6 | Moulding Pressure (Effective) | $P_{eff} = \dfrac{F}{A_{cavity}}$ | MPa | Accounts for projected cavity area |
| 7 | Approximate Shear Rate in Nip | $\dot{\gamma} \approx \dfrac{\pi \times D \times N}{60 \times h}$ | s⁻¹ | h = nip gap (m) |
| 8 | Heat Transfer (Conduction through roll wall) | $Q = \dfrac{k \times A \times \Delta T}{t}$ | W | k = thermal conductivity of steel |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Always use consistent units. If F is in Newtons and A is in mm², then P is in MPa. Never mix N/m² (Pa) with mm² without conversion.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Two Roll Mills** are the **workhorses of the tire industry** — every tire tread compound is mixed on industrial-scale two roll mills before being extruded or calendered.
- **Hydraulic Presses** manufacture **CFRP (Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer)** aircraft components, **phenolic switches**, and **rubber gaskets**.
- **Hand Presses** are still used in **R&D labs, university teaching, and small-batch prototyping** where hydraulic infrastructure is unavailable.
- **Moulds** define the **dimensional accuracy (±0.05 mm)**, **surface finish (Ra 0.2 – 3.2 µm)**, and **production economics** of every plastic/rubber part in the world.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedures & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Standard Operating Procedure (SOP): Two Roll Mill

> [!WARNING]
> **SAFETY FIRST:** Always wear **heat-resistant gloves**, **safety goggles**, and **closed-toe shoes**. Never place hands near the nip while the mill is running. Keep the **emergency stop bar** within reach.

### Step 1: Pre-Operation Checks
- Verify the **roll temperature** is set to the polymer-specific value (e.g., natural rubber: **50–70 °C**).
- Set the **nip gap** using the gap adjustment handwheel (typically **1–3 mm** for mastication).
- Ensure the **cooling water valves** are open (if water-cooled).
- Check **lubrication** of bearings and gear box.

### Step 2: Material Loading
- Weigh the polymer (e.g., **100 g natural rubber**) and additives as per formulation.
- Start the mill. Allow rolls to reach set temperature.
- Feed the polymer into the nip in **small increments** (≈ 1/5 of total batch at a time).

### Step 3: Mastication / Mixing
- Allow the polymer to band on the front (slower) roll.
- Use a **cutting knife** to repeatedly cut and fold the sheet from the rolling bank (every 30 seconds for 5 minutes).
- Add curatives and accelerators **last** to avoid premature scorching.

### Step 4: Sheet-Out and Cooling
- After uniform mixing (visual homogeneity, ≈ 15–20 minutes), pass the sheet through the nip 2–3 times at the **minimum gap**.
- Cut and remove the sheet. Cool on a **clean, dust-free surface**.

### Step 5: Cleanup
- Stop the mill. Use brass scrapers (NOT steel, to avoid roll damage) to remove residual polymer.
- Wipe rolls with solvent-soaked cloth.

## 3.2 Standard Operating Procedure: Hydraulic Press

### Step 1: Pre-Heating
- Switch on the **platen heaters**. Set temperature (e.g., **150 °C for rubber, 180 °C for phenolic**).
- Allow **20–30 minutes** for thermal equilibrium.

### Step 2: Mould Preparation
- Clean the mould cavity with solvent.
- Apply **mould release agent** (silicone spray or PTFE-based) lightly.
- Weigh the correct amount of compound (typically **mould cavity volume × polymer density × 1.05** for 5% flash allowance).

### Step 3: Loading
- Place the compound in the cavity.
- Close the mould and insert it between the platens.

### Step 4: Pressing Cycle
- **Close the press** (manual or automatic).
- **Apply pressure gradually:** Vent at **0.5 MPa** for 30 s to release trapped air, then ramp to the **moulding pressure** (e.g., **10–15 MPa** for rubber).
- Hold for the **cure time** (e.g., **t₉₀ from rheometer data**).

### Step 5: De-moulding
- Release pressure slowly to avoid part distortion.
- Open the press. Use **brass ejector pins** to remove the part.
- Inspect for flash, short-shot, or defects.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 2-Mark Question)

> **Problem:** A hydraulic press has a plunger diameter of **100 mm**. The pressure gauge reads **20 MPa**. Calculate the clamping force developed.

**Given:**
- Plunger diameter, $D = 100$ mm
- Pressure, $P = 20$ MPa = 20 N/mm²

**To Find:** Clamping force $F$ in kN.

**Solution:**

The cross-sectional area of the plunger is:

$$
A = \frac{\pi \times D^{2}}{4}
$$

Substituting the value of $D = 100$ mm:

$$
A = \frac{\pi \times (100)^{2}}{4}
$$

Computing the numerator first:

$$
\pi \times 10000 = 31415.93
$$

Now divide by 4:

$$
A = \frac{31415.93}{4} = 7853.98 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

Now apply Pascal's law. The force equals pressure times area:

$$
F = P \times A
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
F = 20 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \times 7853.98 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

Multiplying:

$$
F = 157079.6 \text{ N}
$$

Converting to kilonewtons (1 kN = 1000 N):

$$
F = \frac{157079.6}{1000} = 157.08 \text{ kN}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{F \approx 157.1 \text{ kN} \approx 15.7 \text{ Ton-force}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer Insight:** A 20 MPa gauge pressure on a 100 mm plunger gives ~157 kN — a typical 4-post lab press generates **50–500 kN**, so this is within operational range.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Equipment

| Task | Best Equipment | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Mixing rubber with carbon black | Two Roll Mill | High shear, no melt required |
| Compression molding a phenolic gear | Hydraulic Press + Compression Mould | High tonnage, heated platens |
| Making a small tensile specimen (lab) | Hand Press + Dumbbell Mould | Low cost, low tonnage sufficient |
| Mass-producing 10,000 PET bottles | Injection Moulding Machine | Cycle time < 30 s per part |
| Vulcanizing a thick rubber bushing | Hydraulic Press (large tonnage) | Even pressure distribution |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Two Roll Mill — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Motor Drive 5 to 25 HP] --> B[Gear Reduction Box]
    B --> C1[Back Roll Faster]
    B --> C2[Front Roll Slower]
    C1 --> D[Nip Gap 0.5 to 5 mm]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E[Rolling Bank]
    E --> F[Polymer Sheet Output]
    G[Heating System] --> C1
    G --> C2
    H[Cooling Water Inlet] --> C1
    H --> C2
    I[Emergency Stop Bar] --> A
    J[Operator Cutting Knife] --> E
```

**Process Flow Interpretation:**
- The **Motor Drive** powers both rolls through a **Gear Reduction Box**, which sets the **Friction Ratio (FR = N₁/N₂ = 1.1 to 1.4)**.
- The **Heating System** (electric/steam) and **Cooling Water Inlet** maintain the roll surface temperature within ±2 °C of the setpoint.
- The **Rolling Bank** is the rotating mass of polymer between the two rolls where the actual mixing occurs.
- The **Emergency Stop Bar** is a horizontal bar across the front of the mill — pushing it stops the rolls instantly.

## 4.2 Hydraulic Press — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Electric Motor 3 to 10 HP] --> P2[Hydraulic Pump]
    P2 --> P3[Pressure Relief Valve]
    P2 --> P4[Directional Control Valve]
    P4 --> P5[Lower Platen Cylinder]
    P5 --> P6[Ram Piston]
    P6 --> P7[Lower Platen Heated]
    P4 --> P8[Upper Platen Fixed Heated]
    P7 --> P9[Mould Cavity]
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10[Pressure Gauge 0 to 35 MPa]
    P11[Reservoir Oil] --> P2
    P2 --> P11
    P12[Thermocouple] --> P7
    P12 --> P8
    P13[Temperature Controller] --> P12
```

**Process Flow Interpretation:**
- The **Hydraulic Pump** draws oil from the **Reservoir** and pressurizes it.
- The **Directional Control Valve** directs oil to either the **Ram Piston** (compression) or back to the reservoir (retraction).
- The **Pressure Gauge** displays the actual line pressure; the **Pressure Relief Valve** prevents over-pressurization.
- The **Thermocouples** in both platens feed back to the **Temperature Controller** for closed-loop heating.

## 4.3 Hand Press — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[Manual Lever Arm] --> H2[Screw Jack]
    H2 --> H3[Platen]
    H3 --> H4[Mould]
    H5[Fixed Frame] --> H6[Bottom Platen]
    H4 --> H6
    H1 --> H7[Locking Pin]
    H8[Optional Cartridge Heater] --> H3
    H8 --> H6
```

**Process Flow Interpretation:**
- The operator pulls down the **Manual Lever Arm**, which rotates the **Screw Jack**.
- The screw converts rotational motion into linear motion, pushing the **Platen** down onto the **Mould** in the **Fixed Frame**.
- The **Locking Pin** holds the lever in place during the cure cycle.

## 4.4 Mould Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M0[Polymer Moulds] --> M1[Compression Mould]
    M0 --> M2[Transfer Mould]
    M0 --> M3[Injection Mould]
    M1 --> M1a[Open Flash Type]
    M1 --> M1b[Positive Type]
    M1 --> M1c[Semi Positive Type]
    M2 --> M2a[Plunger Type]
    M2 --> M2b[Plunger and Disc Type]
    M3 --> M3a[Two Plate]
    M3 --> M3b[Three Plate]
    M3 --> M3c[Hot Runner]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank, Module 2 Lab]**
*Define a Two Roll Mill. State any three operating parameters that affect the quality of mixing.*

**Model Answer:**

A Two Roll Mill is a polymer/rubber processing machine consisting of two counter-rotating heated rolls with an adjustable nip gap, used to plasticize, mix, and homogenize polymer compounds.

The three key operating parameters are:

1. **Friction Ratio** (1.1–1.4) — governs the shear intensity and rolling bank formation.
2. **Roll Temperature** (30–150 °C) — controls viscosity and prevents scorching.
3. **Nip Gap** (0.5–5 mm) — determines the shear rate; smaller gap = higher shear.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse "nip gap" with "roll gap" — the nip is the *narrowest contact point*, while the gap refers to the adjustable distance between roll centers.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
*State Pascal's Law. How is it applied in a hydraulic press?*

**Model Answer:**

**Pascal's Law (1653):** Pressure applied to an enclosed, incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished throughout the fluid in all directions.

**Application in Hydraulic Press:** A small force $F_1$ applied to a small piston of area $A_1$ creates a pressure $P = F_1/A_1$ that is transmitted to a larger piston of area $A_2$, producing a multiplied force $F_2 = P \times A_2$. This allows the press to generate clamping forces of 50–5000 kN with modest pump input.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to state that the fluid must be **incompressible** and the system **leak-free** for Pascal's law to hold exactly.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose Either A or B)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model ESE Question, Module 2 Lab, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Two Roll Mill**. State the role of the **friction ratio** and **nip gap** in determining mixing efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic press has a plunger diameter of **150 mm** and is used to mould a rubber sheet of projected cavity area **10,000 mm²**. The moulding pressure required is **12 MPa**. Calculate:
- (i) The force on the plunger.
- (ii) The clamping force on the cavity, and state whether the press is suitable. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for A(a):

**Construction:**

1. **Two horizontal rolls** (diameter 150–250 mm, length 300–500 mm) made of chilled cast iron or hardened steel, with internal bores for heating/cooling.
2. **Bearing housings** supporting each roll in a heavy cast-iron frame.
3. **Drive system:** electric motor + gearbox + coupling, with one roll driven directly and the other via the gear ratio (this sets the friction ratio).
4. **Nip adjustment handwheel** to set the gap.
5. **Emergency stop bar** for safety.
6. **Temperature control panel** for roll heating.

**Working:**

- The polymer is fed into the nip. It sticks to the slower (front) roll due to higher friction and is peeled off by the faster (back) roll.
- A **rolling bank** forms between the rolls, where shear deformation and folding occur.
- The operator uses a **cutting knife** to repeatedly cut the sheet, fold it, and feed it back — ensuring homogenization.
- Additives (curatives, fillers) are added sequentially and mixed uniformly.

**Role of Friction Ratio:**

- $FR = N_1/N_2 = 1.1$ to $1.4$
- A higher FR creates stronger shear but may overheat the compound.
- Typical FR for natural rubber mixing: **1.25**.

**Role of Nip Gap:**

- Smaller gap → higher shear rate, faster mixing, but more heat generation.
- Larger gap → lower shear, used for sheet-out and final homogenization.

---

#### Model Solution for A(b):

**Given:**
- Plunger diameter, $D = 150$ mm
- Cavity area, $A_c = 10000$ mm²
- Moulding pressure, $P = 12$ MPa = 12 N/mm²

**(i) Force on the plunger:**

Plunger area:

$$
A_p = \frac{\pi \times D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (150)^2}{4}
$$

Computing:

$$
A_p = \frac{\pi \times 22500}{4} = \frac{70685.83}{4} = 17671.46 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

Force on plunger:

$$
F_p = P \times A_p = 12 \times 17671.46 = 212057.5 \text{ N} \approx 212.1 \text{ kN}
$$

**(ii) Clamping force on the cavity:**

$$
F_c = P \times A_c = 12 \times 10000 = 120000 \text{ N} = 120 \text{ kN}
$$

**Suitability Check:**

The plunger force (212.1 kN) must exceed the cavity clamping force (120 kN) to ensure complete mould closure.

Since **212.1 kN > 120 kN**, the press is **suitable** for the operation.

$$
\boxed{F_p \approx 212.1 \text{ kN}, \quad F_c = 120 \text{ kN}, \quad \text{Press is suitable.}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model ESE Question, Module 2 Lab, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Compare **Compression Moulds**, **Transfer Moulds**, and **Injection Moulds** in terms of feeding mechanism, complexity, cycle time, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a functional block diagram, describe the construction and working of a **Hand Press**. A hand press has a lever arm of length **400 mm** and a screw of mean radius **20 mm** with a single-start square thread of lead **5 mm** and friction angle **6°**. Calculate the **ideal mechanical advantage** of the screw. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for B(a):

| Parameter | Compression Mould | Transfer Mould | Injection Mould |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feeding | Polymer placed directly in cavity | Polymer melted in pot, transferred via plunger | Molten polymer injected through sprue |
| Cavity Complexity | Simple | Moderate | Highly complex |
| Cycle Time | Long (5–20 min) | Medium (1–5 min) | Very short (10–60 s) |
| Wastage | High (flash) | Medium | Low (sprue & runner) |
| Applications | Rubber gaskets, phenolic parts, brake pads | Electrical switches, intricate thermosets | Thermoplastics, mass production |

---

#### Model Solution for B(b):

**Construction of Hand Press:**

1. Heavy **cast-iron frame** (C-shape or H-frame).
2. **Fixed lower platen** and **movable upper platen**.
3. **Screw jack** (single-start square thread) connecting the lever to the upper platen.
4. **Manual lever** for applying force.
5. **Locking pin / ratchet** to maintain pressure.
6. Optional **cartridge heaters** for temperature control.

**Working:**

- The polymer charge is placed in the mould on the lower platen.
- The operator pulls the lever, rotating the screw.
- The screw converts rotation into linear downward motion, pressing the upper platen onto the mould.
- The locking pin holds the lever, maintaining pressure during cure.
- After curing, the lever is released and the part is ejected.

**Ideal Mechanical Advantage (IMA) of the Screw:**

The ideal MA of a screw is the ratio of the distance traveled by the lever (in one full rotation) to the linear advance of the platen (the lead of the thread).

Distance traveled by the lever per rotation:

$$
d_{lever} = 2 \pi \times L
$$

where $L$ is the lever arm length (400 mm).

$$
d_{lever} = 2 \pi \times 400 = 2513.27 \text{ mm}
$$

Linear advance of the platen per rotation = lead of the thread, $l = 5$ mm.

Ideal Mechanical Advantage:

$$
IMA = \frac{d_{lever}}{l} = \frac{2 \pi \times L}{l}
$$

Substituting:

$$
IMA = \frac{2 \pi \times 400}{5} = \frac{2513.27}{5} = 502.65
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{IMA \approx 503}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This means a **10 kg hand force** can theoretically generate **10 × 503 = 5030 kg ≈ 5 tonnes** of compression force. In practice, the actual MA is lower due to thread friction, typically 30–50% of the IMA.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in This Module:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency in Pascal's Law Problems** — Mixing N/m² with mm² without conversion. Always convert MPa to N/mm² (they are equivalent!).
> 2. **Confusing Plunger Force with Cavity Force** — They are NOT the same. The plunger force acts on the hydraulic ram; the cavity force is pressure × cavity area.
> 3. **Forgetting the "Incompressibility" condition** when stating Pascal's Law.
> 4. **Mixing up Roll Speeds** — The front roll is the **slower** one (higher friction, more adhesion).
> 5. **Ignoring Friction in Screw MA** — The ideal MA calculation neglects friction; if asked for actual MA, you must include the friction angle term $\tan(\alpha)$ where $\alpha$ is the helix angle, not the friction angle (common confusion).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (High-Yield):**

- **Two Roll Mill** uses **friction ratio (1.1–1.4)** and **nip gap (0.5–5 mm)** to generate shear mixing.
- **Front roll = slower**, back roll = faster; polymer bands on the front roll.
- **Hydraulic Press** is governed by **Pascal's Law**: $P = F/A$ with pressure in **MPa** and area in **mm²**.
- **Hand Press** uses a **screw-and-lever** system; Ideal MA = $2\pi L / l$.
- **Compression Mould** = direct charge in cavity; **Transfer Mould** = charge in pot; **Injection Mould** = molten polymer injected.
- **Cure time** for thermosets/rubber is determined by **rheometer (t₉₀)** data.
- **Mould release agent** is essential to prevent sticking.
- **Always vent** the press at low pressure first to release trapped air.
- **Safety gear**: gloves, goggles, closed shoes, and a clear emergency stop access.
- **Formula to memorize cold:** $F = P \times A$, $FR = N_1/N_2$, $IMA = 2\pi L / l$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Aim of the Experiment
To develop experimental skill in **measuring the cure time (vulcanization time) of an elastomeric compound** using a **rheometer**, and to **correlate the effect of mastication time on plasticity and viscosity** of the raw rubber using a **Mooney Viscometer** and/or **Williams Plastimeter**.

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cure Time (t₉₀):**
> The time required for a rubber compound, held at a specified vulcanization temperature (typically **150 °C** or **160 °C**), to reach **90 % of the maximum torque** developed during vulcanization. It represents the **optimum cure time** — the point at which the desired cross-link density and physical properties are achieved.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scorch Time (t₂ or t₅):**
> The time at which the torque rises **2 (or 5) Mooney units above the minimum torque** during a cure test. It represents the **induction period** before vulcanization actually starts — i.e., the safe processing window before the compound begins to cross-link prematurely.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mastication:**
> A **mechanical–thermal process** in which raw (unvulcanized) rubber is subjected to **shear, compression, and friction** in a two-roll mill or internal mixer. The primary objective is to **reduce the molecular weight (chain length)** of the polymer, lower its viscosity, and make it amenable to subsequent compounding and shaping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plasticity (Williams Plasticity Number – P):**
> A numerical measure of the **deformability / softness** of raw rubber, determined by compressing a cylindrical specimen of fixed dimensions between two parallel plates under a constant load for a fixed time and temperature. It is **inversely related to Mooney viscosity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mooney Viscosity (ML 1+4 at 100 °C):**
> The **shear torque** (in Mooney units, MU) required to rotate a serrated disc embedded in a rubber sample at **2 rpm** (1 minute preheat + 4 minutes running) at **100 °C**, enclosed in a die cavity. Standard reference: **ISO 289 / ASTM D1646**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bread-Kneading" Model

Imagine a baker preparing **bread dough**:
- **Mastication** is like **kneading dough** — the more you knead, the smoother, softer, and easier to shape the dough becomes. In rubber, mechanical shear breaks long polymer chains (chain scission), reducing viscosity and increasing plasticity.
- **Cure (vulcanization)** is like **baking the dough** — once heat is applied, the soft workable material is "locked in" via cross-links into a permanently elastic solid. **Cure time** is exactly how long you must bake to get the perfect loaf.
- **Scorch time** is the warning before baking — the time dough can sit at room temperature before it starts hardening (pre-cross-linking). If you wait too long, you can't shape it.

In short: **Mastication = softening phase**, **Cure = hardening phase**.

## 1.4 Standard Test Conditions (Bold Constants)

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Mastication temperature** | **30 – 70 °C** (room to warm roll) |
| **Mooney test temperature** | **100 °C** (also 125 °C for some rubbers) |
| **Cure test temperature** | **150 °C, 160 °C, or 180 °C** |
| **Mooney rotor speed** | **2 rpm (0.21 rad/s)** |
| **Disc oscillation angle (ODR)** | **± 1° or ± 3°** |
| **ODR frequency** | **1.667 Hz (100 cpm)** |

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Cure Time Measurement

### 2.1.1 Vulcanization (Cross-Linking) Chemistry
When an elastomer is compounded with **sulfur + accelerators + activators (ZnO/stearic acid)** and heated, three-dimensional **sulfur cross-links** form between adjacent polymer chains:

$$
\text{Polymer chain}—\text{S}_x—\text{Polymer chain} \quad (x = 1 \text{ to } 8)
$$

As cross-link density increases:
- **Modulus ↑**, **Elastic recovery ↑**, **Permanent set ↓**
- **Viscosity / plasticity ↓** during cure (initially) then torque rises sharply.

### 2.1.2 The Cure Curve — Torque vs. Time (Rheometer Trace)

A typical **Oscillating Disc Rheometer (ODR)** trace has three regions:

```
Torque
  │
  │           ┌────────────── M_HF (Maximum torque)
  │          ╱
  │         ╱
  │        ╱
  │   ────┘ ← t₂ (Scorch)
  │  M_L (Minimum torque)
  └─────────────────────────────► Time
        0      t₉₀
```

- **M_L** → Minimum torque (viscosity of unvulcanized compound)
- **M_HF** → Maximum (plateau) torque (fully cured)
- **t₂** → Scorch time (early cure onset)
- **t₉₀** → Optimum cure time
- **Cure Rate Index (CRI)** → a measure of cure speed

### 2.1.3 Why Mastication Affects Plasticity and Viscosity

Mastication introduces **mechano-chemical chain scission** in the polymer. As mastication time (t_m) increases:

$$
\bar{M}_n \downarrow \quad \Rightarrow \quad \eta \downarrow \quad \Rightarrow \quad P \uparrow
$$

where $\bar{M}_n$ is the number-average molecular weight, $\eta$ is melt viscosity, and $P$ is plasticity number. The relationship is governed (approximately) by the **Mastication Kinetics Equation**:

$$
\frac{1}{\bar{M}_n(t)} - \frac{1}{\bar{M}_n(0)} = k_m \, t_m
$$

where $k_m$ is the rate constant of mechano-chemical degradation (depends on shear rate, temperature, oxygen presence).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Form | Units / Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Optimum cure time | $t_{90}$ | minutes |
| 2 | Scorch time | $t_2$ (or $t_5$) | minutes — processing safety |
| 3 | **Cure Rate Index (CRI)** | $CRI = \dfrac{100}{t_{90} - t_2}$ | min⁻¹ |
| 4 | Maximum torque | $M_{HF}$ (or $M_{H}$) | dN·m (ODR) |
| 5 | Minimum torque | $M_L$ | dN·m |
| 6 | Torque difference (extent of cure) | $\Delta M = M_{HF} - M_L$ | dN·m |
| 7 | Mooney viscosity | $ML(1+4)\text{ at }100\,°C$ | Mooney Units (MU) |
| 8 | Williams Plasticity Number | $P = h \times 100$ | dimensionless ×10⁻² mm |
| 9 | **Plasticity Retention Index (PRI)** | $PRI = \dfrac{P_{aged}}{P_{fresh}} \times 100$ | % — oxidation resistance |
| 10 | Mastication kinetics | $\dfrac{1}{\bar{M}_n(t)} - \dfrac{1}{\bar{M}_n(0)} = k_m t_m$ | — |
| 11 | Mooney → viscosity conversion (approx.) | $\eta \approx ML \times k$ | Pa·s |
| 12 | Cross-link density (Flory–Rehner) | $\nu = \dfrac{-\ln(1-V_r) + V_r + \chi V_r^2}{V_s \left(V_r^{1/3} - \dfrac{V_r}{2}\right)}$ | mol/cm³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall Avoidance:** In KTU valuation, students often confuse **t₉₀ (cure time)** with **scorch time t₂**. Remember: *t₂* is the start, *t₉₀* is the optimum end. CRI uses the **difference** in denominator.

## 2.3 Engineering Significance

- **Cure time** dictates **molding cycle times** → directly impacts production cost.
- **Scorch time** governs **scorch safety** in extrusion, calendering, and transfer molding.
- **Plasticity / Mooney viscosity** determines **compounding, extrusion, and calendering** ease.
- The **mastication–plasticity correlation** is the basis for industrial **mastication control charts** ensuring batch-to-batch uniformity.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Cure Rate Index (CRI)

The **Cure Rate Index (CRI)** is defined by ASTM D2084 / D5289 as the slope of the steepest portion of the cure curve, expressed as:

$$
CRI = \frac{100}{t_{90} - t_2} \quad \left[\text{min}^{-1}\right]
$$

### Step-by-step logical development:

**Step 1 — Identify the two reference points on the rheometer trace.**

The lower reference is $t_2$ — the time at which torque first rises 2 dN·m above $M_L$ (or 5 dN·m, depending on standard).

The upper reference is $t_{90}$ — the time at which torque reaches:

$$
M(t_{90}) = M_L + 0.90 \, (M_{HF} - M_L)
$$

**Step 2 — Express the effective cure duration.**

$$
\Delta t_{cure} = t_{90} - t_2
$$

This is the "useful cure window" — the time during which the compound transitions from scorch onset to 90 % cure completion.

**Step 3 — Convert to a rate.**

A higher $CRI$ value means the cure window is shorter (faster curing). To make higher = faster, take the reciprocal and scale by 100:

$$
CRI = \frac{100}{\Delta t_{cure}} = \frac{100}{t_{90} - t_2}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example.**

Suppose for an SBR compound at 160 °C:

$$
t_2 = 2.5 \text{ min}, \quad t_{90} = 8.5 \text{ min}
$$

$$
\Delta t_{cure} = 8.5 - 2.5 = 6.0 \text{ min}
$$

$$
CRI = \frac{100}{6.0} = 16.67 \text{ min}^{-1}
$$

**Interpretation:** A compound with CRI ≈ 17 cures relatively fast — suitable for high-volume compression molding.

## 3.2 Derivation of Mastication Kinetics

The mechanical chain scission during mastication follows first-order kinetics with respect to the number of chain scissions per unit mass.

**Step 1 — Define chain scission rate.**

Let $N(t)$ = number of polymer chains per unit mass at time $t$. Each chain scission adds one new chain:

$$
\frac{dN}{dt} = k_m \, N(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Solve the ODE.**

$$
\int_{N_0}^{N(t)} \frac{dN}{N} = \int_0^{t_m} k_m \, dt
$$

$$
\ln \frac{N(t)}{N_0} = k_m t_m
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to molecular weight.**

Since $\bar{M}_n = \dfrac{m_{\text{total}}}{N}$, we have $N \propto \dfrac{1}{\bar{M}_n}$:

$$
\ln \frac{\bar{M}_n(0)}{\bar{M}_n(t)} = k_m t_m
$$

Exponentiating and rearranging:

$$
\frac{1}{\bar{M}_n(t)} = \frac{1}{\bar{M}_n(0)} + \frac{k_m t_m}{\text{const.}}
$$

Leading to the linear form used industrially:

$$
\frac{1}{\bar{M}_n(t)} - \frac{1}{\bar{M}_n(0)} = K \, t_m
$$

where $K = k_m / \text{(mass per chain)}$.

**Step 4 — Link to Mooney Viscosity.**

Mooney viscosity correlates empirically with molecular weight via a power law:

$$
ML(1+4) = K_M \, \bar{M}_n^{a}
$$

where $a \approx 3.4$ for many elastomers. Thus:

$$
ML(t_m) \propto \left[\frac{1}{\bar{M}_n(0)} + K t_m\right]^{-3.4}
$$

As $t_m \uparrow$, $\bar{M}_n \downarrow$, hence $ML \downarrow$.

## 3.3 Analytical Correlation Matrix — Mastication Time vs. Property

| Mastication time (tₘ, min) | Molecular weight $\bar{M}_n$ | Mooney viscosity (MU) | Williams Plasticity | Processability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 (unmasticated) | Very high | Very high (often > 100) | Very low (brittle) | Poor |
| 5 | High | High (80–90) | Low | Moderate |
| 15 | Moderate | Medium (50–60) | Medium | Good |
| 30 | Low | Low (30–40) | High (soft) | Excellent |
| 60 (over-masticated) | Too low | Too low (< 25) | Sticky / tacky | Poor (lacks green strength) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off:** Excessive mastication gives soft, easy-to-process rubber but **destroys green strength** and mechanical properties. An optimum tₘ exists for each elastomer (e.g., NR ≈ 10–20 min, SBR ≈ 5–10 min, BR ≈ 5 min — synthetic rubbers already have lower initial $\bar{M}_n$).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Full KTU-Style Problem

**Problem:** A natural rubber sample was masticated for varying times and tested at 100 °C. Results:

| tₘ (min) | ML(1+4) (MU) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 95 |
| 5 | 78 |
| 10 | 64 |
| 15 | 53 |
| 20 | 45 |
| 25 | 39 |

A second sample, after 10 min mastication, was cured at 160 °C in an ODR. Results: $M_L = 6$ dN·m, $M_{HF} = 42$ dN·m, $t_2 = 3.0$ min, $t_{90} = 12.0$ min.

**Calculate (a) CRI, (b) Mooney viscosity drop per minute of mastication, (c) the cross-link density equivalent.**

### Solution:

**(a)** From the formula:

$$
CRI = \frac{100}{t_{90} - t_2} = \frac{100}{12.0 - 3.0} = \frac{100}{9.0} = 11.11 \text{ min}^{-1}
$$

**(b)** Average drop in Mooney viscosity per minute (between tₘ = 0 and 25 min):

$$
\text{Slope} = \frac{95 - 39}{25 - 0} = \frac{56}{25} = 2.24 \text{ MU/min}
$$

So mastication reduces viscosity by about **2.24 Mooney units per minute**.

**(c)** Torque rise during cure:

$$
\Delta M = M_{HF} - M_L = 42 - 6 = 36 \text{ dN·m}
$$

Using the empirical correlation $\nu \approx \Delta M / (2 \times F \times R)$ where $F$ and $R$ are constants of the die geometry, the **relative cross-link density** is proportional to $\Delta M$. Therefore $\Delta M = 36$ dN·m indicates a **moderately high cross-link density**, suitable for products like tire treads or industrial hoses.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define cure time (t₉₀) and scorch time (t₂). Why is the difference (t₉₀ − t₂) industrially important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Cure time (t₉₀):** Time required for the compound to reach 90 % of the difference between $M_{HF}$ and $M_L$ in an oscillating disc rheometer. It represents the **optimum vulcanization point** (1 mark).
- **Scorch time (t₂):** Time at which torque rises **2 dN·m** above $M_L$. Represents the **pre-cure induction period** (1 mark).
- **Industrial importance:** The difference $(t_{90} - t_2)$ defines the **useful cure window** and is used to calculate the **Cure Rate Index (CRI)**. A small difference = fast cure = high production rate but low scorch safety; a large difference = better scorch safety but slower production. The optimum balance is selected based on the molding method (1 mark).

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**How does mastication affect Mooney viscosity and Williams plasticity of natural rubber? Mention the underlying mechanism.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- During mastication on a two-roll mill, **mechano-chemical chain scission** occurs, breaking long polymer chains (1 mark).
- As mastication time increases, $\bar{M}_n$ **decreases**, hence **Mooney viscosity decreases** (becomes softer) and **Williams plasticity number increases** (rubber deforms more easily) (1 mark).
- The relationship follows approximately $ML \propto \bar{M}_n^{3.4}$ and $1/\bar{M}_n - 1/\bar{M}_n(0) = K t_m$ (1 mark).

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe with a neat labeled diagram the construction and working of an **Oscillating Disc Rheometer (ODR)**. Explain how **t₂, t₉₀, M_L, and M_{HF}** are obtained from the rheogram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a rubber compound cured at 160 °C, the following data were obtained:
$M_L = 8$ dN·m, $M_{HF} = 48$ dN·m, $t_2 = 2.8$ min, $t_{90} = 11.2$ min.
Calculate (i) **Cure Rate Index**, (ii) **% cure at t = 6 min**, (iii) the **processing safety window** if the upper limit for scorch is $t_2 + 4$ min. Comment on the suitability of this compound for **injection molding**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) ODR Construction and Working (7 marks):**

**Construction:**
- A **die assembly** consisting of upper and lower platens, both electrically heated (to 160 °C) with RTD sensors and PID controllers.
- A **serrated biconical disc rotor** (the oscillating disc) embedded in the lower die cavity.
- A **drive mechanism** with an eccentric cam imparting ± 1° (or ± 3°) oscillation at **1.667 Hz**.
- A **torque transducer / strain gauge** on the rotor shaft to measure resistance torque.
- A **data logger / plotter** producing the cure curve (M vs. t).

**Working:**
- A **disc-shaped rubber specimen** (~8 cm³) is placed in the lower die cavity and closed by the upper die under light pressure.
- The rotor oscillates; the **viscous + elastic resistance** of the curing rubber creates a restoring torque on the shaft.
- As vulcanization progresses, cross-links form → torque rises.
- The plot of torque vs. time gives the rheogram.

**Rheogram interpretation (labels on the y-axis torque, x-axis time):**
- $M_L$ — minimum torque (read at the initial flat trough)
- $t_2$ — time at $M_L + 2$ dN·m
- $M_{HF}$ — maximum (plateau) torque
- $t_{90}$ — time at $M_L + 0.90(M_{HF} - M_L)$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) CRI:**

$$
CRI = \frac{100}{t_{90} - t_2} = \frac{100}{11.2 - 2.8} = \frac{100}{8.4} = 11.90 \text{ min}^{-1}
$$

**(ii) % cure at t = 6 min:**

At t = 6 min, the torque will have risen from $M_L$ towards $M_{HF}$. Assuming linear rise in the steep region (between t₂ = 2.8 min and t₉₀ = 11.2 min):

$$
\text{Slope} = \frac{M_{HF} - M_L}{t_{90} - t_2} = \frac{48 - 8}{8.4} = 4.76 \text{ dN·m/min}
$$

At t = 6 min, torque reached:

$$
M(6) = M_L + \text{slope} \times (6 - t_2) = 8 + 4.76 \times 3.2 = 8 + 15.24 = 23.24 \text{ dN·m}
$$

Cure percentage:

$$
\%\text{ cure} = \frac{M(6) - M_L}{M_{HF} - M_L} \times 100 = \frac{15.24}{40} \times 100 = 38.1\%
$$

So at 6 min, the compound is only ~38 % cured.

**(iii) Processing safety window:**

$$
\text{Safety window} = (t_2 + 4) - t_2 = 4 \text{ min}
$$

The compound can be safely processed (extruded, injected) within the first 4 minutes after heating without premature vulcanization.

**Suitability for injection molding:**
- CRI = 11.90 min⁻¹ → moderately fast cure (good for injection molding which requires short cycle times).
- Scorch safety = 4 min → reasonable but **marginal** for very fast injection cycles.
- **Verdict:** The compound is **suitable** for injection molding with appropriate barrel temperature control. Recommended mold cure time ≈ t₉₀ = 11.2 min, with a small additional post-cure margin of 1–2 min.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe the **Mooney Viscometer test** with reference to standard (ML 1+4 at 100 °C). Explain how **Mooney viscosity, Mooney scorch time, and Δ torque** are determined. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Natural rubber was masticated on a two-roll mill for varying times. The Williams plasticity numbers obtained are:

| tₘ (min) | 0 | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| P (×10⁻² mm) | 28 | 42 | 56 | 68 | 76 | 82 |

Plot the **P vs. tₘ curve**, calculate the **plasticity retention** if the rubber aged for 24 h at 70 °C and the aged plasticity was 60. Determine the **optimum mastication time** if the desired P is **65 ± 5**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Mooney Viscometer Test (7 marks):**

- **Apparatus:** Motor-driven serrated disc rotor inside a heated die cavity (temperature controlled ± 0.5 °C).
- **Sample:** Two pieces of rubber (~25 cm³ total) placed above and below the rotor, closed in the die.
- **Test conditions:** ML (1+4) at 100 °C → 1 minute preheat + 4 minutes of running at **2 rpm**.
- **Mooney viscosity (ML):** The torque (in Mooney Units, MU) is read at the end of 4 min running. Typical NR ranges 40–90 MU.
- **Mooney scorch time (t₅):** Run the test at higher temperature (e.g., 125 °C); record the time for torque to rise 5 MU above the minimum.
- **Δ torque (ΔM):** Difference between maximum torque during run and minimum — indicates extent of cross-linking during the test itself.

**(b) Numerical/Graphical Solution:**

- **Plot P vs. tₘ:** Draw curve rising from 28 → 82 monotonically, showing saturation (diminishing slope) after ~20 min.
- **Plasticity Retention Index (PRI):**

$$
PRI = \frac{P_{aged}}{P_{fresh}} \times 100 = \frac{60}{P_{fresh}} \times 100
$$

$P_{fresh}$ from the table depends on the mastication state — typically taken at tₘ = 10 min (P = 56) for NR testing per **ISO 2930**:

$$
PRI = \frac{60}{56} \times 100 = 107.1\%
$$

(A PRI > 100 is possible if aging causes further plasticization, though ideally PRI is close to 100 for good oxidation resistance.)

- **Optimum mastication time for P = 65 ± 5:**
  - Target range: P = 60 to 70.
  - From the data: P = 68 at tₘ = 15 min ✓ (within 60–70).
  - **Optimum tₘ ≈ 15 min.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing t₂ and t₅** — some standards use t₅ (5 dN·m rise). Always mention which you are using.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract M_L** when computing % cure — many students write $(M - M_{HF})/M_{HF}$ which is wrong.
> 3. **Inverting CRI formula** — CRI = 100/(t₉₀ − t₂), NOT 100/(t₂ − t₉₀). Numerator is **always 100** (a convention, not a torque value).
> 4. **Mooney test — forgetting the 1-minute preheat** — the "1" in (1+4) is preheat, not part of the formula; otherwise students miss the meaning of standard.
> 5. **Williams plasticity unit confusion** — P is dimensionless numerically equal to **height × 100 in 0.01 mm**. Don't write units of mm directly.
> 6. **Cure temperature** — always state it (commonly **160 °C**); omitting it loses a mark.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cure time t₉₀** = time to reach 90 % of maximum torque on the rheometer curve; **scorch time t₂** = time for 2 dN·m rise above minimum.
- **CRI = 100 / (t₉₀ − t₂)** — higher CRI → faster cure → better for high-volume molding but poorer scorch safety.
- **Mooney Viscosity (ML 1+4 at 100 °C)** measures the resistance of raw/compounded rubber to shearing at 100 °C using a serrated rotor at 2 rpm.
- **Williams Plasticity Number P = h × 100** measures compressibility (softness) — **increases with mastication**.
- **Mastication causes mechano-chemical chain scission** → $\bar{M}_n$ ↓ → ML ↓ → P ↑.
- **Kinetic relation:** $\dfrac{1}{\bar{M}_n(t)} - \dfrac{1}{\bar{M}_n(0)} = K t_m$ — linear in mastication time.
- **Optimum mastication** balances processability (low ML, high P) against green strength and final mechanical properties.
- **Trade-off:** Over-masticated rubber becomes sticky, has poor dimensional stability and low tensile strength.
- **Test instruments required for the lab:** Two-roll mill, Mooney viscometer, Oscillating Disc Rheometer (or Moving Die Rheometer), Williams Plastimeter, stopwatch, die cutters.
- **Always record:** Temperature, sample mass, preheat time, rotor speed, and final numerical values with units.
- **Standard references:** **ISO 289** (Mooney), **ISO 6502 / ASTM D2084** (ODR cure), **ISO 2930** (PRI of NR), **ASTM D1646** (rubber viscosity).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Topic:** Build Skills to Manufacture Various Rubber and Plastic Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — BUILD SKILLS TO MANUFACTURE VARIOUS RUBBER AND PLASTIC PRODUCTS

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 What is "Manufacture of Rubber and Plastic Products"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> *Manufacturing of rubber and plastic products* refers to the systematic conversion of raw elastomers (natural rubber, synthetic rubbers) and thermoplastic/thermosetting polymers — together with their compounding ingredients — into finished, dimensionally stable, and functionally useful industrial or consumer articles using controlled **shaping**, **forming**, and **crosslinking/setting** operations.

In KTU's **PCPOL508 — Polymer Technology Laboratory / Polymer Processing** framework, this experiment is designed to build **hands-on (psycho-motor)** skills in:

- **Compounding** (weighing, mixing, mastication, blending)
- **Shaping** (molding — compression, injection, transfer; extrusion; calendering; casting; blow molding; thermoforming)
- **Vulcanization / Curing** (for rubbers) and **Solidification / Cooling** (for plastics)
- **Finishing & Testing** (trimming, post-cure, hardness, tensile, swelling)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Model" 🍰

Think of polymer manufacturing exactly like **baking a cake**:

| Stage | Kitchen Analogy | Polymer Reality |
|---|---|---|
| **Raw materials** | Flour, sugar, eggs | Polymer resin + additives |
| **Mixing** | Whisking batter | Two-roll mill / internal mixer |
| **Shaping** | Pouring into a cake tin | Compression / injection mold |
| **Setting** | Baking in oven | Vulcanization (rubber) or cooling (plastic) |
| **Finishing** | Icing, decorating | Trimming, polishing, testing |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as no cake can be baked without first mixing the ingredients in the right proportion, no polymer product can be made without proper **compounding** — the heart of polymer processing.

### 1.3 Classification of Manufactured Products — Two Big Families

**A. Rubber (Elastomeric) Products**
> Defined by **ASTM D1566** as *a material that is capable of recovering from large deformations quickly and forcibly, and that is essentially insoluble in boiling solvents like benzene, methyl ethyl ketone, or ethanol-toluene azeotrope.*

- Tyres, tubes, conveyor belts, hoses, gaskets, O-rings, rubber bands, erasers, shoe soles, rubber sheets, rubberized fabrics, rubber rollers.

**B. Plastic (Polymeric) Products**
> Defined as *materials that contain, as an essential ingredient, one or more high-molecular-weight organic polymers, are solid in their finished state, and at some stage in their manufacture or processing into finished articles can be shaped by flow.*

- Bottles, films, bags, pipes, sheets, cups, toys, switchboards, buckets, fibres, containers.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics & Definitions

- **Mooney Viscosity (ML₁₊₄ at 100°C)** — *measures rubber processability*. Standard unit: **MU (Mooney Units)**.
- **Scorch Time (tₛ₂ or t₅)** — *time before vulcanization begins*. Standard: **minutes**.
- **Cure Time (t₉₀)** — *time to achieve 90% of maximum torque*. Standard: **minutes**.
- **Melt Flow Index (MFI / MFR)** — *mass of polymer flowing in 10 min under specified load & temperature*. Standard: **g/10 min**.
- **Shore A Hardness** — *indentation hardness of soft elastomers*. Range: **0–100**.
- **Tensile Strength** — *maximum stress a sample can withstand while being stretched*. Standard: **MPa**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always **report** the *test temperature*, *load*, and *specimen geometry* alongside these metrics, as KTU viva questions frequently target these "hidden" parameters.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Polymer Manufacturing

The entire manufacturing flow rests on **three sequential pillars**:

### Pillar I — Compounding (Formulation Design)

A polymer is almost **never** used in its virgin form. The classic formulation architecture is:

1. **Base Polymer / Elastomer** (100 phr) — the matrix
2. **Fillers** (0–150 phr) — reinforcement, cost reduction (e.g., carbon black, silica, calcium carbonate)
3. **Plasticizers / Softeners** (0–50 phr) — processability (e.g., DOP, naphthenic oils)
4. **Curing / Crosslinking Agents** (1–5 phr) — vulcanization (e.g., **sulfur**, peroxides)
5. **Accelerators** (0.5–2 phr) — speed up cure (e.g., **MBT, CBS, TMTD**)
6. **Activators** (3–10 phr) — boost accelerators (e.g., **ZnO + stearic acid**)
7. **Antioxidants / Antidegradants** (1–3 phr) — service life (e.g., **IPPD, 6PPD, TMQ**)
8. **Processing Aids** (0.5–5 phr) — mould release, tack (e.g., waxes, factice)
9. **Specialty Additives** — colourants, flame retardants, antistatic, blowing agents

> [!NOTE]
> The notation **phr** means **parts per hundred parts of rubber** (or resin). It is the universal language of polymer formulation, and **KTU exams almost always** test the student's ability to **scale a given formula to a specific batch size**.

### Pillar II — Shaping (Converting the Compound into a Form)

Rubber and plastics use overlapping but distinct shaping techniques:

| Process | Domain | Principle | Typical Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Compression Molding** | Rubber / Thermoset | Compound placed in hot mold, pressure closes mold, cure occurs | O-rings, seals, gaskets |
| **Injection Molding** | Rubber / Thermoplastic | Compound injected into closed mold cavity | Soles, intricate rubber/plastic parts |
| **Transfer Molding** | Rubber / Thermoset | Pre-heated compound forced through a sprue into closed mold | Insulators, brush holders |
| **Extrusion** | Rubber / Thermoplastic | Compound forced through a die of fixed cross-section | Pipes, tubes, profiles, sheets |
| **Calendering** | Rubber / Plastic (PVC) | Compound passed between heated rollers | Films, sheets, coated fabrics |
| **Blow Molding** | Thermoplastic | Air inflates a parison inside a mold cavity | Bottles, containers |
| **Thermoforming** | Thermoplastic (sheet) | Heated sheet stretched onto/into a mold | Trays, cups, blister packs |
| **Casting** | PU / Epoxy / Nylon | Liquid resin poured into a mold, cures at room temp | PU rollers, nylon rods |
| **Hand Lay-up** | FRP / GRP | Resin-impregnated fibres laid in mold | Boat hulls, tanks |

### Pillar III — Crosslinking / Solidification (Locking the Shape)

| Material | Mechanism | Conditions | Key Variable |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Natural Rubber / SBR / NBR (sulfur cured)** | Sulfur bridges between C=C bonds | 140–170 °C, 5–30 min | **Crosslink density** |
| **EPDM / Silicone (peroxide cured)** | Free-radical C–C bridges | 160–180 °C, 5–20 min | **Peroxide loading** |
| **Thermoplastics** | Physical cooling below Tg/Tm | Ambient / chilled water | **Cooling rate** |
| **Thermosets (PF, MF, UP, EP)** | Irreversible chemical cure | Heat + catalyst | **Gel time** |

> [!NOTE]
> A **thermoplastic** is *reprocessable* (the crosslinking is physical — chain entanglements) while a **thermoset / vulcanized rubber** is *non-reprocessable* (the crosslinks are chemical — covalent). KTU exams love this distinction.

## 2.2 The Vulcanization Reaction — The Heart of Rubber Manufacturing

For a **sulfur-cured diene rubber**, the crosslinking reaction is:

$$\text{Polymer–CH}_2\text{–CH=CH–CH}_2\text{–Polymer} \;+\; \text{S}_8 \;\xrightarrow{\,\text{Accelerator + ZnO + Stearic acid, 140-170°C}\,}\; \text{Polymer–CH}_2\text{–CH(S}_x\text{)–CH}_2\text{–Polymer}$$

where $x$ = 1 (monosulfidic), 2 (disulfidic), or higher (polysulfidic) bridges.

> [!TIP]
> A **monosulfidic bridge** is short and thermally stable (high heat aging resistance). A **polysulfidic bridge** is long and gives better dynamic fatigue (high flex resistance). This trade-off is a **classic 3-mark KTU question**.

### Role of the Activator System (ZnO + Stearic Acid)

$$\text{ZnO} + 2\,\text{Stearic Acid} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Zinc Stearate} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Zinc stearate is **soluble in the rubber matrix** and acts as the actual catalyst for sulfur crosslinking. Without it, vulcanization is impractically slow.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **phr to mass conversion** | $m_i = \dfrac{W_{batch}}{100 + \sum m_{phr,i}} \times m_{phr,i}$ | grams (g) | Scale-up of any recipe |
| 2 | **Mooney–Rivlin elasticity** (rubber) | $\sigma = 2\left(C_1 + \dfrac{C_2}{\lambda}\right)\left(\lambda - \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}\right)$ | Pa | Tensile behaviour of elastomers |
| 3 | **Melt Flow Index (MFR)** | $\text{MFR} = \dfrac{m \times 600}{t}$ | g / 10 min | Plastics processability |
| 4 | **Cure rate index (CRI)** | $\text{CRI} = \dfrac{100}{t_{90} - t_{s2}}$ | % / min | Vulcanization efficiency |
| 5 | **Crosslink density (Flory–Rehner)** | $\nu_e = \dfrac{-\ln(1 - v_r) + v_r + \chi v_r^2}{V_1 \left(v_r^{1/3} - \dfrac{v_r}{2}\right)}$ | mol / cm³ | Swelling test analysis |
| 6 | **Volume fraction of rubber in swollen gel** | $v_r = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{\rho_r}{\rho_s}\!\left(\dfrac{W_s - W_d}{W_d}\right)}$ | — | Swelling test analysis |
| 7 | **Shore A → Young's modulus (approx.)** | $E \,(\text{MPa}) \approx \dfrac{0.0981 \times (145 - \text{ShoreA})}{0.939 \, \text{ShoreA} - 71.2}$ | MPa | Hardness → stiffness |
| 8 | **Heat generation in mastication** | $Q = \eta \, \dot{\gamma}^{\,2} \, V \, t$ | J | Two-roll mill energy |
| 9 | **Theoretical cure time** | $t_{90} = \dfrac{\ln(0.1)}{-k}$ (first-order approx.) | min | Cure kinetics |
| 10 | **Shrinkage allowance (rubber mold)** | $L_{cavity} = L_{part} \times (1 + S)$ | mm | Mold design |

> [!NOTE]
> The two **most-tested** formulas in KTU PCPOL508 Module 2 are: **(1) the phr scaling formula** and **(2) the cure rate index (CRI)**. Memorize them with units and limitations.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

| Skill Built | Industrial Use |
|---|---|
| Compression / injection molding of rubber | Tyre retreading, gasket production, O-ring manufacturing |
| Two-roll mill mastication & mixing | All rubber goods industries — base step for every rubber product |
| Extrusion of thermoplastics | PVC pipe industries, HDPE pipe industries, packaging film lines |
| Blow molding of PET/HDPE | Beverage bottle industry (Coca-Cola, Bisleri) |
| Compression molding of thermosets | Electrical switchgear (Bakelite, SMC, DMC), brake pads |
| Calendering of PVC | Vinyl flooring, tarpaulin, blood bags |
| Hand lay-up of GRP | Wind turbine blades, boat hulls, swimming pools |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP PROCEDURES & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example A — Manufacture of a **Rubber Eraser** (Compression Moulded, Sulfur Cured)

### 3.1.1 Recipe (Industrial-Style, Total Batch 500 g)

| Ingredient | Role | phr | Mass for 500 g batch (g) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Natural Rubber (RSS-5) | Base elastomer | 100 | 250.00 |
| Zinc Oxide (ZnO) | Activator | 5 | 12.50 |
| Stearic Acid | Co-activator | 2 | 5.00 |
| Carbon Black (HAF, N330) | Reinforcing filler | 40 | 100.00 |
| Sulphur | Crosslinker | 8 | 20.00 |
| MBT (Mercaptobenzothiazole) | Accelerator | 0.8 | 2.00 |
| Process Oil (Naphthenic) | Plasticizer | 5 | 12.50 |
| BHT (Butylated Hydroxy Toluene) | Antioxidant | 1 | 2.50 |

**Total phr = 100 + 5 + 2 + 40 + 8 + 0.8 + 5 + 1 = 161.8 phr**

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Calculation Using the phr Formula

$$m_{NR} = \dfrac{500}{161.8} \times 100 = 308.9 \text{ g}$$

$$m_{ZnO} = \dfrac{500}{161.8} \times 5 = 15.45 \text{ g}$$

$$m_{\text{Stearic Acid}} = \dfrac{500}{161.8} \times 2 = 6.18 \text{ g}$$

$$m_{CB} = \dfrac{500}{161.8} \times 40 = 123.6 \text{ g}$$

$$m_{S} = \dfrac{500}{161.8} \times 8 = 24.7 \text{ g}$$

$$m_{MBT} = \dfrac{500}{161.8} \times 0.8 = 2.47 \text{ g}$$

$$m_{\text{oil}} = \dfrac{500}{161.8} \times 5 = 15.45 \text{ g}$$

$$m_{BHT} = \dfrac{500}{161.8} \times 1 = 3.09 \text{ g}$$

$$\text{Check Sum} = 308.9 + 15.45 + 6.18 + 123.6 + 24.7 + 2.47 + 15.45 + 3.09 \approx 499.8 \text{ g} \;\; ✓$$

> [!NOTE]
> The sum should equal the target batch size within rounding error. If it doesn't, **recompute** — this is a very common KTU valuation check.

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Manufacturing Procedure

**Step 1 — Mastication**
- Set the **two-roll mill** to a friction ratio of **1 : 1.25** (front roll 18 rpm, back roll 22 rpm).
- Set roll temperature to **40–50 °C**.
- Pass natural rubber through the nip **5–7 times** until a smooth band forms on the front roll.
- *Objective:* Reduce Mooney viscosity from ~80 MU to ~50 MU, improving processability.

**Step 2 — Sequential Addition of Ingredients (in order)**
1. **Stearic acid** → 2 min
2. **ZnO** → 2 min
3. **MBT (accelerator)** → 1 min
4. **Carbon black** — *add in 3 small portions* → 5 min
5. **Process oil** → 2 min
6. **Antioxidant (BHT)** → 1 min
7. **Sulphur** — *added LAST to avoid "scorching"* → 1.5 min

> [!WARNING]
> **Sulphur must always be added LAST** during mixing because premature addition at high temperature causes **scorch** — premature vulcanization inside the mill, ruining the batch. This is a top KTU viva question.

**Step 3 — Sheeting & Cooling**
- Sheet out at a thin gauge (~2 mm) and cool on a clean, dust-free surface.
- Age the sheet for **at least 24 hours** at room temperature before molding to allow complete diffusion of curatives.

**Step 4 — Compression Molding**
- Mould temperature: **150 ± 5 °C**
- Pressure: **10–15 MPa**
- Curing time: **8–12 minutes** (typically 1 min per mm thickness)
- Pre-heat the mould for 5 min before placing the blank.

**Step 5 — Demoulding, Trimming & Inspection**
- Open the mould carefully (use ejector pins).
- Trim the flash with a sharp knife or scissors.
- Inspect for **under-cure** (tacky, soft), **over-cure** (brittle, cracked), or **porosity** (bubbles).

### 3.1.4 Quality Tests (Mandatory in KTU Record)

| Test | Method | Acceptance |
|---|---|---|
| Hardness | Shore A durometer (5 s reading) | 50 ± 5 Shore A |
| Tensile strength | Dumbbell, ASTM D412 | ≥ 10 MPa |
| Elongation at break | Same as above | ≥ 300 % |
| Visual | Naked eye / hand lens | No flash, no bubbles |

## 3.2 Worked Example B — Manufacture of a **Plastic Sheet by Compression Molding (HDPE)**

### 3.2.1 Procedure

1. Weigh **HDPE granules** (e.g., 100 g).
2. Pre-heat the **hydraulic press** platens to **160 °C ± 5 °C**.
3. Place a **mould-release agent** (silicone spray) on the mould cavity.
4. Charge the HDPE granules into the **square cavity mould** (e.g., 150 × 150 × 3 mm).
5. Close the press; apply **5 MPa** pressure.
6. Hold for **5 minutes** at temperature and pressure (allows melting and flow).
7. Apply **15 MPa** during the next **3 minutes** (consolidation).
8. Cool to **40 °C** under pressure (maintain pressure to avoid warping and sink marks).
9. Release pressure, open mould, demould, trim edges.
10. Test: **MFR**, **tensile strength**, **impact strength**, **visual inspection**.

## 3.3 Worked Example C — Manufacture of a **PVC Pipe by Extrusion**

### 3.3.1 Procedure

1. **Dry blend** the PVC compound: PVC resin (100 phr) + stabilizer (3 phr) + lubricant (1.5 phr) + processing aid (2 phr) + impact modifier (5 phr) + TiO₂ (4 phr) + CaCO₃ (10 phr).
2. Feed the dry blend into the **hopper** of a single-screw extruder.
3. Set **barrel temperatures** in 4 zones: **160 / 170 / 175 / 180 °C**.
4. Set **die temperature** to **185 °C**; **screw speed** to **30 rpm**.
5. PVC melt emerges from the die as a continuous pipe.
6. Pass through a **vacuum calibration tank** (cools and sizes the pipe).
7. Pass through a **water-spray cooling tank**.
8. **Haul-off** unit pulls at constant speed; **cutter** cuts to required length.
9. Test: **dimensional accuracy**, **impact resistance (falling weight)**, **burst pressure**, **density**.

## 3.4 Worked Example D — Manufacture of a **PET Bottle by Blow Molding**

### 3.4.1 Procedure

1. **Dry the PET** at **160 °C for 4–6 hours** (moisture → hydrolysis → degradation).
2. Inject a molten PET **preform** into a steel mold (injection unit).
3. Cool the preform to ~ ambient, transfer to the **blow-mold station**.
4. **Re-heat** the preform with infrared heaters to ~100 °C.
5. Place in the blow mold; insert a **stretch rod** axially.
6. Inject **compressed air at 0.7–1.0 MPa** — the preform inflates and conforms to the mold.
7. Hold for **3–5 s** for cooling and setting.
8. Open mould, remove bottle, trim flash.
9. Test: **drop test**, **burst pressure**, **top-load strength**, **wall thickness distribution**.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Process

| If the product is… | Best Process | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| A small rubber O-ring | Compression molding | Low cost, simple tool, good for thermoset |
| A tyre tread | Extrusion + calendaring then cure | Continuous profile, high volume |
| A 3D toy (plastic) | Injection molding | Complex shape, high repeatability |
| A 1-litre water bottle | Blow molding (extrusion or injection) | Hollow thin-walled |
| A boat hull (large) | Hand lay-up with GRP | Low tooling cost, large size |
| A PVC pipe | Extrusion | Continuous length, constant cross-section |
| A switchboard (Bakelite) | Compression molding | Thermoset, large flat area |
| A plastic shopping bag | Film extrusion (blown film) | Thin, flexible, continuous |
| A PU foam cushion | Casting (foam rise) | Liquid resin expands in situ |
